abstract.para: The &cockpit; web console allows you to view, filter, and manage virtual machines (VMs), storage pools, and networks. You can also use this interface to import existing VMs and activate, deactivate, and delete your virtualization resources.

topic.para: You can dynamically modify the VM through &cockpit;. You can add disks to the VM for additional block storage for data isolation or LVM expansion, add network interfaces to enable traffic segregation between public and private networks, add host devices to allow the VM to access physical hardware directly, and add shared directories to facilitate file transfers between the host and guest file systems. You can also take snapshots before and in between to create a recovery point, ensuring the VM can be restored if a configuration change leads to a boot failure or OS corruption.

section.title: Modifying virtual machine
section.para: To modify a virtual machine's configuration,

step.para: Navigate to the Virtual machines page.
Click the required VM name.
Adjust the VM attributes as described in the following sections.

section.title: Modifying basic VM attributes
section.para: In the Overview section, you can change the following attributes of the VM:

listitem.para: To modify the Memory, click edit and change the storage and memory, and then click Save.
To modify the Boot order, click edit and select the up and down arrows to set the boot order, and then click Save.
To enable or disable running the VM when the host boots, click the Autostart toggle button.
To modify the Watchdog device type, click edit, select the action, and then click Save.
Watchdogs act when systems stop responding. To use this virtual watchdog device, the guest system also needs to have an additional driver and a running watchdog service.
The available options are:
Reset performs a hard reset of the VM.
Power off shuts down the VM. Use this if a hung VM might cause data corruption on shared storage.
Inject a non-maskable interrupt when the watchdog timer expires. The hypervisor sends a non-maskable interrupt (NMI) signal to the virtual processor.
Pause suspends the VM in its current state. This allows an administrator to verify the VM for debugging before manual recovery.
Do nothing records the event in the host logs but takes no physical action on the VM.
To modify the Vsock, click edit and modify or remove the custom identifier.
Virtual socket support enables communication between the host and guest over a socket. It still requires special vsock-aware software to communicate over the socket.
To modify the Firmware, click the firmware name, select the required firmware from the drop-down list, and click Save.

section.title: Managing disks
section.para: You can add a new disk to the VM, add an existing disk, or attach a virtual disk that already exists as a file on the host machine’s file system.

step.para: In the Disks section, click Add disk.
To create a new disk source, select Create new in Source.

listitem.para: Select the pool name from the Pool drop-down list.
Specify the new volume name in the Name field.
Select the disk size and format.
Click Show additional options and select the cache and bus from the drop-down lists.
The available options for cache are:
None: Bypass the guest's cache; the VM talks directly to the host's storage.
Writeback: Writes data to the host's page cache and reports it as done immediately.
Writethrough: Writes data to both the cache and the disk before the write is confirmed.
DirectSync: This is similar to None but bypasses even more layers of the host's file system cache. This is useful for specific database workloads that handle their own caching.
The Bus type determines the protocol used to communicate between the VM and the storage.
The following bus options are available:

VirtIO: VirtIO is a paravirtualization standard. Instead of the host machine pretending to be a specific piece of physical hardware (which is slow), the guest OS uses a special driver that knows it is running in a virtual environment. This is best used for standard production and high-performance.

SATA/IDE: These emulate older physical hardware. Use these only if you are installing an older OS (like Windows XP) that doesn't have VirtIO drivers out of the box. This can be used for OS installation or troubleshooting.

SCSI: Often used in enterprise environments, specifically with the virtio-scsi controller, which allows for advanced features like TRIM/Discard (to reclaim space on SSDs). This can be used for enterprise storage or databases.

USB: The USB option allows you to pass through physical devices from your host machine (like a thumb drive, webcam, or security dongle) directly into the VM. Use USB only when you need to connect a specific physical device or if you are troubleshooting a system that cannot load VirtIO drivers.
Specify the disk identifier in Disk identifier.

step.para: To use an existing disk source, select Use existing in Source.

listitem.para: Select the pool name from the Pool drop-down list.
Select the new volume name from the Volume drop-down list.
Click Show additional options and select the cache and bus from the drop-down lists.
The available options for cache are:
None: Bypass the guest's cache; the VM talks directly to the host's storage.
Writeback: Writes data to the host's page cache and reports it as done immediately.
Writethrough: Writes data to both the cache and the disk before the write is confirmed.
DirectSync: This is similar to None but bypasses even more layers of the host's file system cache. This is useful for specific database workloads that handle their own caching.
The Bus type determines the protocol used to communicate between the VM and the storage.
The available options for bus are:

VirtIO: VirtIO is a paravirtualization standard. Instead of the host machine pretending to be a specific piece of physical hardware (which is slow), the Guest OS uses a special driver that knows it is running in a virtual environment. This is best used for standard production and high-performance.

SATA/IDE: These emulate older physical hardware. Use these only if you are installing an older OS (like Windows XP) that doesn't have VirtIO drivers out of the box. This can be used for OS installation or troubleshooting.

SCSI: Often used in enterprise environments, specifically with the virtio-scsi controller, which allows for advanced features like TRIM/Discard (to reclaim space on SSDs). This can be used for enterprise storage or databases.

USB: The USB option allows you to pass through physical devices from your host machine (like a thumb drive, webcam, or security dongle) directly into the VM. Use USB only when you need to connect a specific physical device or if you are troubleshooting a system that cannot load VirtIO drivers.
Specify the disk identifier in Disk identifier.

step.para: To use a custom path for the disk source, select the Custom path option in Source.

listitem.para: Select the custom path from the Custom path drop-down list.
Select the device as Disk image file or CD/DVD disc from the Device drop-down list.
Click Show additional options and select the cache and bus from the drop-down lists.
The available options for cache are:
None: Bypass the guest's cache; the VM talks directly to the host's storage.
Writeback: Writes data to the host's page cache and reports it as done immediately.
Writethrough: Writes data to both the cache and the disk before the write is confirmed.
DirectSync: This is similar to None but bypasses even more layers of the host's file system cache. This is useful for specific database workloads that handle their own caching.
The Bus type determines the protocol used to communicate between the VM and the storage.
The available options for bus are:

VirtIO: VirtIO is a paravirtualization standard. Instead of the host machine pretending to be a specific piece of physical hardware (which is slow), the Guest OS uses a special driver that knows it is running in a virtual environment. This is best used for standard production and high-performance.

SATA/IDE: These emulate older physical hardware. Use these only if you are installing an older OS (like Windows XP) that doesn't have VirtIO drivers out of the box. This can be used for OS installation or troubleshooting.

SCSI: Often used in enterprise environments, specifically with the virtio-scsi controller, which allows for advanced features like TRIM/Discard (to reclaim space on SSDs). This can be used for enterprise storage or databases.

USB: The USB option allows you to pass through physical devices from your host machine (like a thumb drive, webcam, or security dongle) directly into the VM. Use USB only when you need to connect a specific physical device or if you are troubleshooting a system that cannot load VirtIO drivers.
Specify the disk identifier in Disk identifier.

step.para: Click Add.
The new disk has been added to the virtual machine.
In the Disks section, click Edit corresponding to the disk you want to modify.
Select the access type as Read-only or Writeable.
Select the cache and bus from the drop-down lists.
The available options for cache are:

listitem.para: None: Bypass the guest's cache; the VM talks directly to the host's storage.
Writeback: Writes data to the host's page cache and reported as "done" immediately.
Writethrough: Writes data to both the cache and the disk before the write is confirmed.
DirectSync: This is similar to None but bypasses even more layers of the host's file system cache. This is useful for specific database workloads that handle their own caching.

step.para: The Bus type determines the protocol used to communicate between the VM and the storage.
The available options for bus are:

listitem.para: VirtIO: VirtIO is a paravirtualization standard. Instead of the host machine pretending to be a specific piece of physical hardware (which is slow), the Guest OS uses a special driver that knows it is running in a virtual environment. This is best used for standard production and high-performance.

SATA/IDE: These emulate older physical hardware. Use these only if you are installing an older OS (like Windows XP) that doesn't have VirtIO drivers out of the box. This can be used for OS installation or troubleshooting.

SCSI: Often used in enterprise environments, specifically with the virtio-scsi controller, which allows for advanced features like TRIM/Discard (to reclaim space on SSDs). This can be used for enterprise storage or databases.

USB: The USB option allows you to pass through physical devices from your host machine (like a thumb drive, webcam, or security dongle) directly into the VM. Use USB only when you need to connect a specific physical device or if you are troubleshooting a system that cannot load VirtIO drivers.

step.para: Click Save.
The attributes are updated.
In the Disks section, click the three-dot icon corresponding to the disk you want to remove.
Click Remove.
To detach the disk from the VM so the disk is not visible to the guest OS, click Remove.
To detach the disk from the virtual machine and delete the corresponding file on the VM host, click Remove and delete file.

section.title: Managing network interfaces
section.para: You can add a new virtual NIC to your VM using one of the following network interfaces:

listitem.para: Virtual network, the recommended type for general guest connectivity on hosts with dynamic / wireless networking configurations. Provides a connection whose details are described by the named network definition.

Bridge to LAN, the recommended type for general guest connectivity on hosts with static wired networking configurations. Provides a bridge from the guest virtual machine directly onto the LAN. This needs a bridge device on the host with one or more physical NICs.
Direct attachment, the recommended type for high performance or enhanced security.

step.para: In the Network interfaces section, click Add network interface
Select the interface type as Virtual network, Bridge to LAN, or Direct attachment from the drop-down list.

listitem.para: For a Virtual network:
Select the source from the Source list.
Select the model from the Model list.
To generate the MAC address automatically, in MAC address, select Generate automatically.
To set the MAC address manually, in MAC address, select Set manually, and specify the required MAC address.
For a Bridge to LAN:
Select the model from the Model list.
If you want to generate the MAC address automatically, in MAC address, select Generate automatically.
If you want to set the MAC address manually, in MAC address, select Set manually, and specify the required MAC address.
For a Direct attachment, select Direct attachment and select the source from the Source list.
Select the Mode as VEPA, Bridge, Private, or Passthrough.
For more information on mode, see .
Select the model from the Model list.
To generate the MAC address automatically, in MAC address, select Generate automatically.
To set the MAC address manually, in MAC address, select Set manually, and specify the required MAC address.

step.para: Click Add.
The new network interface has been added to the virtual machine.
In the Network interfaces section, click Edit corresponding to the network interface you want to modify.
Select the interface type from the drop-down list.
For a Virtual network, select Virtual network.

listitem.para: Select the source from the Source list.
Select the model from the Model list.
Modify the MAC address.

step.para: For a Bridge to LAN, select Bridge to LAN.

listitem.para: Select the model from the Model list.
Modify the MAC address.

step.para: For a Direct attachment, select Direct attachment and select the source from the Source list.

listitem.para: Select the Mode as VEPA, Bridge, Private, or Passthrough.
For more information on mode, see .
Select the model from the Model list.
Modify the MAC address.

step.para: Click Save.
The network interface is updated.

section.title: Plugging or unplugging network interfaces
section.para: You can plug or unplug network interfaces from the virtual machine while troubleshooting or to ensure security. You can also unplug a virtual network interface to move it to a different VLAN or bridge without shutting down the entire networking service.
In the Network interfaces section, click the Plug or Unplug button corresponding to the network interface.
The network interface is plugged into or unplugged from the virtual machine as selected.

step.para: In the Network interfaces section, click the three-dot icon corresponding to the network interface you want to remove.
Click Remove.
The network interface is removed from the virtual machine.

section.title: Managing host devices
section.para: You can add host devices to give the VM direct access to physical hardware currently plugged into the host.

step.para: In the Host devices section, click Add host device.
Select the host device type as USB or PCI.
Select the required devices to be added from the given list.
Click Add.
The selected host devices are added to the virtual machine.

section.title: Removing host devices
section.para: In the Host devices section, click Remove corresponding to the host device you want to remove.

section.title: Snapshots
section.para: A snapshot captures the state of a virtual machine at a specific point in time, providing a recovery point for the operating system and its data. Using snapshots, you can revert to the configuration that was set when the snapshot was taken.

step.para: In the Snapshots section, click Create snapshot.
Modify the snapshot name if required.
The default snapshot name is the date and time at which the snapshot was taken.
Enter a description for the snapshot.
Click Create.
A snapshot is created with the given name and description.

section.title: Deleting or reverting snapshots
section.para: You can revert or delete snapshots. Reverting to the snapshot takes the VM back to the time of the snapshot and the current state is lost, along with any data not captured in a snapshot. When you delete a snapshot, the snapshot and all of its captured content are lost.
To revert a snapshot, click Revert corresponding to the snapshot you want to revert and click Revert to confirm.
To delete a snapshot, click Delete corresponding to the snapshot you want to delete and click Delete to confirm.

section.title: Managing shared directories
section.para: Shared host directories must be manually mounted inside the VM. You can mount the shared folder using the command mount -t virtiofs hostshare [mount point] in which the mount point is the mount point inside the guest.

step.para: Add the shared host directories manually:
In this, the mount tag is a unique name you give the shared folder so the VM can identify it, while the mount point is the actual folder path inside the VM where those files will appear.
In the Shared directories section, click Add shared directory.
Select the host path that is to be exported from the Source path drop-down list.
Specify the tag name to be used by the guest to mount this export point in Mount tag.
To use extended attributes on files and directories, click Show additional options and select the checkbox.
Click Share.
Click Remove corresponding to the shared directory you want to remove.
Click Remove to confirm removing the shared directory.
Navigate to the Virtual machines page and click the three-dot menu corresponding to the VM.
Click Clone.
Specify a name for the cloned VM and click Clone.
A clone of the VM has been created.
Navigate to the Virtual machines page and click the three-dot menu corresponding to the VM.
Click Delete.
Select the associated storage files to be deleted with the VM and click Delete.
The VM and the associated storage files have been deleted.
Navigate to the Virtual machines page and click the three-dot menu corresponding to the VM.
Click Rename.
Specify the new name and click Rename.

section.title: Importing a virtual machine
section.para: Import a virtual machine if you have a disk image with an already installed system: raw, qcow2 and so on. To import a virtual machine, proceed as follows:

step.para: Navigate to the Virtual machines page and click Import VM.
In the Details section, specify the following details:

listitem.para: Enter a name for the VM.
Select the Connection as either System or User session.

note.para: Click the information icon next to Connection to view details on the available options.

listitem.para: Select the disk image located on the host file system.
Select the appropriate operating system and memory.

step.para: Define the Storage parameters, including the storage limit and memory.
To run the VM after importing, click Import and run.
To edit the VM parameters after importing, click Import and edit.
Modify the parameters and click Import and run.
